summary: Joe Reis reflects on his career trajectory, contrasting the high-friction, one-to-one nature of consulting with the scalable, one-to-many impact of creating books and courses. After the unexpected global success of 'Fundamentals of Data Engineering,' he's shifting focus toward content creation that can reach and help more people simultaneously.
key_points: Consulting provides valuable perspective across companies but is linearly scalable and high-friction
Books designed for longevity should avoid code examples and specific tools that quickly become outdated
The success of 'Fundamentals of Data Engineering' demonstrated the power of one-to-many impact
Creating content (books, courses) offers a flywheel effect where impact leads to more opportunities
money_quotes: Even more challenging with consulting is that your impact is one-to-one, meaning one consultant and one client.
Since there's no recurring revenue, you're constantly chasing new work, 'feeding the beast,' as they say.
You quickly realize that most companies - big or small, startups or massive - are essentially the same. Same personalities, problems, etc.
I hope all of you can find a way to impact more people in a 1:many way. It's gratifying and creates a flywheel where more impact leads to more opportunities to create an even more significant impact.
key_insight: Shifting from one-to-one consulting to one-to-many content creation (books, courses) enables greater impact and creates a self-reinforcing flywheel of opportunities.
